channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Platner’s Democratic Support Evaporates After Sexual Assault Allegation
item.description: Graham Platner, the Democratic Senate candidate in Maine, called the allegation false but said he would “reflect” on his political path forward, as many allies rescinded their endorsements.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has until July 13 to withdraw from the Maine Senate race, and if he does, the state Democratic Party has until July 27 to replace him on the ticket, according to state law.

item.title: In CNN Interview, Woman Details Alleged Sexual Assault by Graham Platner
item.description: “This is something that I tried for many years to forget,” the woman, Jenny Racicot, said on CNN.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has faced calls from Democrats to drop out of the Maine Senate race after an allegation of sexual assault by Jenny Racicot, a woman he once dated.

item.title: Who Might Replace Graham Platner if He Drops Out of the Maine Senate Race?
item.description: Graham Platner can be replaced on the ballot if he withdraws in the next week. If he does, Maine Democrats would face an uncertain two-week race to choose a replacement.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner won the Democratic nomination for Senate in early June, but he could still be replaced on the ballot.

item.title: Trump Wanted a U.S. Soccer Star to Play in the World Cup. FIFA Found a Way.
item.description: There was nothing unusual about a call from President Trump or the reversal of Folarin Balogun’s suspension, said the president of FIFA, Gianni Infantino.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: FIFA’s president, Gianni Infantino, speaking to President Trump during an Oval Office visit in November.

item.title: In NATO’s Next Act, Can Europe Play the Leading Role?
item.description: The focus at a leaders’ summit in Turkey this week will be on building a new model for the alliance, as President Trump pushes to do less and pressures others do more.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Lara Jakes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: NATO leaders at last year’s summit in The Hague, where member states committed to increase spending on defense sharply by 2035.

item.title: Canada Bets on NATO Partners for Its Expanded Submarine Fleet
item.description: In selecting a joint bid from Germany and Norway, Prime Minister Mark Carney is elevating Canada’s naval power and reducing its military and economic dependency on the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Chris Helgren/Reuters
item.media:description: Naval officers from Canada touring a South Korean KSS-III submarine in Esquimalt, British Columbia, in May.

item.title: McConnell Has Been Hospitalized for 3 Weeks, and Aides Won’t Say Why
item.description: Senator Mitch McConnell was admitted to the hospital on June 14, and his office has provided few updates about his condition. Here’s what we know.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mitch McConnell, 84, had in recent months used a wheelchair to travel from his office building to the Capitol, although days before his hospitalization he was able to walk on his own.

item.title: Michigan Senate Primary Gives Democrats Stark Choice Between Progressive and Moderate Candidate
item.description: The state’s important Senate primary just became a head-to-head battle between Abdul El-Sayed, an outspoken left-wing candidate, and Haley Stevens, a moderate backed by party leaders.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times; Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Haley Stevens of Michigan has an advertising edge in the state’s Democratic primary for Senate, but Dr. Abdul El-Sayed has led in polls.

item.title: Inside Trump’s Ideological Fight With the Smithsonian
item.description: Publicly and behind the scenes, the president continues to try to impose his own views of American history and culture, presenting an ongoing challenge to Lonnie Bunch, the institution’s leader.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: National Museum of African American History and Culture
item.media:credit: Lawren Simmons for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and his allies have criticized what they call “improper ideology” in the Smithsonian’s presentation of American history and culture.

item.title: Historians Reject Trump Administration’s Criticism of Smithsonian Museum
item.description: The nation’s largest group of scholars of U.S. history denounced a White House report attacking the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: In a lengthy report released on Saturday, the Trump administration accused the Smithsonian Institution’s National Museum of American History of “extreme political activism.”

item.title: Joining a Human Tide of Mourners Flowing Through Tehran’s Streets
item.description: After days of public mourning ceremonies in the Iranian capital, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s coffin arrived in Tehran’s Azadi Square. This is what I saw covering the miles-long procession on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Emile Ducke
item.media:description: Crowds gathered around the coffin of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Tehran, Iran, on Monday.

item.title: New Strikes on Ships in Strait of Hormuz Test U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire
item.description: A U.S. official said Iranian missiles struck two commercial ships. There was no immediate comment from Iranian officials on the reported attacks.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Qasim Nauman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Vessels anchored near Muscat, Oman, last month.

item.title: New Videos of Charlie Kirk’s Killing Emerge in Court, but Few Can Watch
item.description: On Day 1 of what is expected to be a weeklong hearing, prosecutors played videos of the shooting for a judge, but they were graphic and kept from public view. Erika Kirk and Donald Trump Jr. were in court.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy, Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Kevin Lamarque/Reuters

item.title: Trump Passports Debut, Delighting Some and Dismaying Others
item.description: The Washington Passport Agency started issuing the limited-release documents with President Trump’s image on Monday, catching a few applicants by surprise.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sami Aldawood, of Austin, Texas, traveled to Washington to get one of the new commemorative passports with President Trump’s image. Mr. Aldawood, an immigrant from Iraq, became a U.S. citizen a week ago.

item.title: Trump Tries to Take Credit for Walmart Price Cuts of Beef and Other Products
item.description: The retailer said it will lower prices for beef, produce, drinks, pools, toys, grills and summer clothing.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images

item.title: Key Health Care Panel Is Again Blocked From Meeting Under RFK Jr.
item.description: The United States Preventive Services Task Force has been stymied under Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. canceled meetings of the United States Preventive Services Task Force last July, last November and this March.

item.title: The Chinese Company That Could Start a Trade War With Europe
item.description: Nuctech’s rise from a Chinese state-backed start-up to a global border-security supplier has made it a lightning rod in trade and subsidy disputes.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao

item.title: Samsung Made More Profit Last Quarter Than the Last Two Years Combined
item.description: The A.I. boom has propelled the South Korean tech giant’s earnings, but its shares sank as investors had even loftier expectations.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi and Jason Karaian

item.title: Why A.I. Distillation Has Become a Hot Topic in the Race with China
item.description: U.S. companies complain that competitors in China are unfairly copying their A.I. systems using a technique that has been around for years.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Ariel Davis

item.title: Death Toll From Oppressive Heat in New Jersey May Be as High as 29
item.description: In New York City, emergency rooms last week logged the highest number of patients treated for heat-related illnesses in years.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully and Joseph Goldstein
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Soccer fans sought a cool spot last week at a World Cup match at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.

item.title: Heat Waves Are Taking a Big Toll on Chickens
item.description: The birds are particularly vulnerable to high temperatures, and France’s torrid June took a heavy toll.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan and Léontine Gallois
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: Extreme Heat Hits Europe for the Third Time This Year
item.description: It is continent’s third spell of scorching weather since May. Europe is the world’s fastest-warming continent.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar and Natan Odenheimer
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Confessions II’ Review: Madonna Returns to the Dance Floor
item.description: On “Confessions II,” a successor to her hit 2005 album “Confessions on a Dance Floor,” the pop superstar gives new energy to her origin story and shines light on the troubles she’s danced away.
item.dc:creator: Evelyn McDonnell
item.media:credit: XNY/Star Max and GC Images, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Madonna performing in Times Square in June.

item.title: Lessons From the Graham Platner Disaster
item.description: Many Democrats want representatives as furious as they are. That makes it easier for unfit candidates to escape proper vetting.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: JD Vance Is Worried
item.description: The vice president’s theology comes in book form.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Lozada
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
item.media:credit: Paola Chapdelaine for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Kimmel’ Guest Host Tiffany Haddish Roasts Trump’s Fourth of July Party
item.description: The comedian and actress joked that “there was more people at my bat mitzvah” than at America’s 250th birthday party.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: A Long-Awaited Verdict for Marine Le Pen Could Reshape France’s Politics
item.description: A court will decide on Tuesday whether to uphold Ms. Le Pen’s embezzlement conviction. If it does, the far-right leader will probably be barred from running for the presidency next year.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Tom Nicholson/Reuters
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen met with supporters at a country fair in Liévin, France, on Saturday.
